[Immersive Learning](https://www.talespin.com/reading/the-impact-of-a-virtual-reality-learning-environment-in-education#)
The Impact of a Virtual Reality Learning Environment in Education
=================================================================

August 1, 2023

![Image 11](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f5d06f4562d23388d011e72/653f5a90212887f31d35089a_The%20Impact%20of%20a%20Virtual%20Reality%20Learning%20Environment%20in%20Education%20Featured%20Image.png)

Written by:

Talespin Team

Virtual reality (VR) stands on the cutting-edge of educational innovation, poised to redefine conventional structures of knowledge acquisition. As technology continuously evolves, the role of virtual reality within education has similarly surged, presenting new avenues of experiential learning that possess the potential to radically transform pedagogical models. This article delves into an in-depth exploration of VR's implications within education, noting its capacities to foster engagement, facilitate interactivity and promote learner retention. Furthermore, it explores real-life case studies of VR implementation in diverse learning environments and casts light on the trajectory of this promising technology in the education sector.

Understanding Virtual Reality in Education
------------------------------------------

Virtual reality involves the digital recreation, or complete fabrication, of a real-world or fantastical environment. It is a technology that has revolutionized various sectors, including education. Initially, VR experiences were primarily centered upon gaming, but over time, educators have recognized its potential for enhancing learning.

VR technology equips individuals with the opportunity to engage with immersive, three-dimensional environments. Users can interact with virtual elements, manipulate objects, and explore new worlds. The capacity of VR to deliver such realistic, tangible experiences has prompted educators to consider how it may be utilized to bolster learning, particularly in areas where traditional educational techniques perhaps falter.

### The Evolution of Virtual Reality in Learning

The application of VR in education has undergone significant evolution over the past decade. What started as an interesting accessory within select contexts has now become a mainstream tool for enhancing learning experiences. As our understanding of its potential benefits deepens, VR-related learning strategies have gained traction in classrooms around the world.

![Image 12](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f5d06f4562d23388d011e72/653f5a9949a0fbf7dc266fb7_The%20Impact%20of%20a%20Virtual%20Reality%20Learning%20Environment%20in%20Education%20image%201.png)

One of the key advantages of VR in education is its ability to facilitate laboratory experiments that would otherwise be impractical or impossible. Students can now conduct experiments in a virtual lab, exploring scientific concepts in a safe and controlled environment. This not only enhances their understanding but also sparks their curiosity and enthusiasm for science.

Furthermore, VR has proven to be a valuable tool for studying historical cities and civilizations. Students can immerse themselves in the past, walking through ancient streets, exploring architectural wonders, and witnessing historical events. This hands-on approach to history not only makes the subject more engaging but also helps students develop a deeper appreciation for the past.

Another area where VR has made a significant impact is in special education. It provides a safe and inclusive environment for students with disabilities to learn and interact. For example, VR simulations can help individuals with autism develop social skills by practicing real-life scenarios in a controlled setting. Similarly, students with physical disabilities can experience activities and adventures that may otherwise be inaccessible to them.

As technology continues to advance, the possibilities for VR in education are limitless. From virtual field trips to distant planets, to immersive language learning experiences, VR has the potential to transform the way we teach and learn. By providing students with unique and interactive learning opportunities, VR is paving the way for a more engaging and effective educational experience.

The Advantages of Virtual Reality in Education
----------------------------------------------

### Enhancing Student Engagement

![Image 13](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f5d06f4562d23388d011e72/6531311d7313df59f04b2338_Education%20Training%20Image%201.png)

One of the primary benefits of using VR in education is the heightened sense of engagement that it instills within learners. The immersive nature of VR experiences captures the attention of students, fostering a keen interest in the learning material. In fact, studies indicate that VR can heighten motivation, instill a deeper appreciation of subject matter and facilitate an overall increase in academic performance.

_Related:_[_Level-up Your Scenario-Based Learning With VR_](https://talespin.com/blog-post/level-up-your-scenario-based-learning-with-vr)

Imagine a classroom where students are no longer passive recipients of information but active participants in their own learning journey. With VR, students can step into virtual worlds that bring abstract concepts to life. For example, instead of reading about the solar system, students can explore each planet, feel the gravitational pull, and witness the beauty of the cosmos firsthand. This level of engagement not only makes learning more exciting but also enhances comprehension and critical thinking skills.

Furthermore, VR can provide personalized learning experiences tailored to each student's needs and preferences. By adapting the virtual environment to suit individual learning styles, VR ensures that students stay fully engaged and motivated throughout their educational journey.

### Facilitating Interactive Learning

VR is not a passive medium. It provides students with an interactive learning platform whereby they can actively explore, manipulate, and experience various educational perspectives. This hands-on approach not only keeps students engaged but also solidifies learning, as the brain tends to retain information better when it's linked to physical sensations and experiences.

Imagine a history lesson where students can travel back in time and witness historical events unfold before their eyes. They can walk through ancient ruins, converse with historical figures, and truly immerse themselves in the past. This level of interactivity not only makes learning more enjoyable but also fosters a deeper understanding and empathy for different cultures and time periods.

Moreover, VR can simulate real-world scenarios that are otherwise difficult or dangerous to replicate in a traditional classroom setting. For example, medical students can practice complex surgical procedures in a virtual operating room, allowing them to develop crucial skills without the risk of harming patients. This hands-on experience not only enhances learning but also boosts students' confidence and prepares them for real-world challenges.

### Promoting Retention and Recall

VR's capability to immerse students in the learning material has a pronounced effect on their ability to recall information. The vivid sensory experiences that VR provides can imprint educational content deeper into the memory bank, therefore facilitating better retrieval and retention of information. This can be especially beneficial in complex subjects where visual aids can greatly assist in understanding.

Imagine a biology class where students can explore the human body in 3D, observing the intricate details of organs and systems. By interacting with virtual anatomical models, students can gain a comprehensive understanding of complex biological concepts. This visual and interactive approach not only helps students retain information but also enables them to make connections between different parts of the body, fostering a holistic understanding of human anatomy.

Furthermore, VR can provide opportunities for spaced repetition, a proven technique for enhancing long-term memory. By revisiting virtual environments and practicing concepts over time, students can reinforce their knowledge and ensure that it stays fresh in their minds. This repetitive and immersive learning experience helps students retain information beyond the classroom, leading to better academic performance and a deeper understanding of the subject matter.

Case Studies of Virtual Reality in Learning
-------------------------------------------

### Virtual Reality in Science Education

Quite a number of schools and universities around the world have embraced the benefits of VR in science education. For instance, VR can assist students to better comprehend complex biological systems by allowing them to virtually travel through the human body and see its operation from the inside. Similarly, astronomy students can use VR to explore outer space, visiting distant galaxies and observing celestial phenomena in a way that textbooks simply do not allow.

### Virtual Reality in Language Learning

Language learning offers a fascinating example of VR's potential benefits in education. Immersion in a foreign language environment is often considered the most effective means of developing fluency. VR provides the opportunity for students to experience such immersion without the need for extensive travel, exposing them to the authentic linguistic and cultural nuances they need to master a new language.

The Future of Virtual Reality in Education
------------------------------------------

### Emerging Trends in Virtual Reality Learning

![Image 14](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f5d06f4562d23388d011e72/6347d40eed2bc45f6a564b6d_Scenario_Based_VR_Learning-PWC_VR_Study_2.png)

As with any form of technology, developments in VR are ongoing, and they continue to open up new realms of possibility for educational application. Emerging trends hint at far more immersive, interactive virtual classrooms, which pair AI teaching assistants, dynamic lesson plans, and adaptive learning algorithms for a personalized educational experience.

_Related:_[_Talespin 2023 Predictions for Immersive Learning_](https://talespin.com/blog-post/talespin-2023-predictions-for-immersive-learning)

### Potential Challenges and Solutions

Despite its potential, the incorporation of VR into educational systems is not without challenges. The technology can be costly, and its implementation requires technical know-how. Moreover, questions remain regarding it's long-term effects on physical and mental health. In spite of these hurdles, growing investment and research in VR indicate that solutions are on the horizon, and the immense potential of this technology in education can't be overlooked.

Conclusion: Embracing the Virtual Reality Learning Environment
--------------------------------------------------------------

In conclusion, VR offers numerous benefits for education. Its capacity to engage students, provide interactive learning experiences, and bolster recall makes it an invaluable tool in the modern learning environment. By examining the current applications and future trends of VR, it's clear that the education sector stands on the brink of a revolution in teaching and learning methods. While challenges exist, the wide array of solutions and the compelling evidence in favor of VR bolster the argument for its expanded use. As we continue to explore and understand this technology, we will undoubtedly unlock even more ways in which VR can enhance and enrich the learning environment.

* * *

Frequently Asked Questions
--------------------------

**What are the key benefits of using a virtual reality learning environment?**

Virtual reality (VR) learning environments offer immersive, engaging, and experiential education. They improve knowledge retention, enhance understanding, and enable safe simulations in various fields.

**Can you share examples of successful virtual reality learning environments?**

Virtual reality learning environments have been successfully used in medical training, architecture, and manufacturing to create realistic, hands-on experiences for learners.

**How does VR enhance learning outcomes compared to traditional environments?**

VR offers interactive, multisensory experiences, making learning more engaging and memorable. It promotes active participation and skill development, leading to improved outcomes.

* * *

--- SOURCE 4: Learning effectiveness of immersive virtual reality in education and ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035

SUMMARY:
The study analyzed 30 relevant research articles to compare the relative effectiveness of IVR-based learning and its impact on learner engagement, as outlined in the ICAP framework (Chi & Wylie, 2014). Research on learning with IVR in secondary and higher education suggests several conclusions about the effectiveness of learning in promoting different forms of knowledge, with some evidence that IVR tends to outperform non-immersive technologies in the acquisition of conceptual information and procedural knowledge (ibid.). Makransky, G.B. Petersen The cognitive affective model of immersive learning (CAMIL): A theoretical research-based model of learning in immersive virtual reality  Educational Psychology Review, 33 (3) (2021), pp. 2024, Computers and Education X Reality  Show abstract This study explored the effects of IVR interactivity on learners’ sense of agency, cognitive load, and learning outcomes.

Learning effectiveness of immersive virtual reality in education and training: A systematic review of findings
==============================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Matthias Conrad, David Kablitz, Stephan Schumann

Abstract
--------

The use of [immersive virtual reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/immersive-virtual-reality "Learn more about immersive virtual reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IVR) offers a variety of design possibilities for action-oriented [teaching and learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/teaching-and-learning "Learn more about teaching and learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that enables the promotion of specific knowledge and skills. In order to use [IVR applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/virtual-reality-application "Learn more about IVR applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as an effective teaching and learning medium, it is important to understand the potential advantages and disadvantages of this technology compared to other media. This raises the question of what type of learning environment is most effective in promoting specific knowledge and skills. To answer these questions, a [systematic review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of research on learning with IVR in an educational context was conducted using the PRISMA method (Liberati et al., 2009; Page et al., 2021). The study analyzed 30 relevant research articles to compare the relative effectiveness of IVR-based learning and its impact on [learner engagement](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/learner-engagement "Learn more about learner engagement from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), as outlined in the ICAP framework (Chi & Wylie, 2014). The results indicate that IVR has a positive impact on learning compared to other types of media. The study suggests that [IVR technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/virtual-reality-technology "Learn more about IVR technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is suitable for learning environments that prioritize active learner engagement and practical application, such as active manipulation and constructive creation. In summary, the results offer more insights into the advantages of using IVR to accomplish particular learning objectives.

1. Introduction
---------------

The use of [immersive virtual reality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/immersive-virtual-reality "Learn more about immersive virtual reality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IVR) enables complex instructional arrangements that can promote the achievement of specific learning goals. In the area of education and training, particular potential is seen in the promotion of procedural knowledge required to solve complex problems in novel or unfamiliar work contexts ([Hamilton et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib15)). Research on learning with IVR in secondary and higher education suggests several conclusions about the effectiveness of learning in promoting different forms of knowledge, with some evidence that IVR tends to outperform non-immersive technologies in the acquisition of conceptual information and procedural knowledge (ibid.). Under certain conditions, the use of IVR has also been found to be conducive to the acquisition of [declarative knowledge](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/declarative-knowledge "Learn more about declarative knowledge from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Hamilton et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib15); [Jensen & Konradsen, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib18)). Other studies find an advantage in achieving cognitive learning goals when using IVR, as well as greater [learner engagement](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/learner-engagement "Learn more about learner engagement from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Concannon et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib12)). This paper compares the benefits of this technology in terms of [learning outcomes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/learning-experiences "Learn more about learning outcomes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with the use of other types of media. It also explores what type of learning environment is most effective in promoting specific knowledge and skills when using [IVR technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/virtual-reality-technology "Learn more about IVR technologies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Understanding the benefits of using this technology in terms of learning outcomes, especially in comparison to alternative media, is of [paramount importance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/paramount-importance "Learn more about paramount importance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the field of [computer assisted learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/computer-assisted-learning "Learn more about computer assisted learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and instruction. In addition, it is imperative for educators and educational institutions to understand the particular domain of knowledge that is best advanced through the integration of IVR in the field of Computer Assisted Learning and Instruction. A nuanced understanding of the specific benefits of IVR enables educators to tailor instructional strategies to create immersive and engaging [learning experiences](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/learning-experience "Learn more about learning experiences from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that are tailored to the preferences of the learner. It helps ensure that instructional content is seamlessly aligned with the inherent strengths of this technology. Such precision in approach is critical to increasing the overall impact on learning outcomes. By identifying the specific types of learning environments in which IVR is superior, educators gain the ability to create compelling and memorable learning experiences that increase student engagement and retention. Maintaining an awareness of the distinct advantages offered by IVR is critical to keeping educational institutions abreast of technological advancements in this rapidly evolving landscape. Such awareness not only ensures technological relevance, but also serves as a catalyst for continued research and innovation in the field of [educational technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/educational-technology "Learn more about educational technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This, in turn, facilitates the development and refinement of novel [IVR applications](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/virtual-reality-application "Learn more about IVR applications from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and learning experiences. The main [added value](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/value-added "Learn more about added value from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of this study lies in the study-based identification of the relative superiority of IVR-based learning environments in terms of specific forms of learning, with special consideration of the associated learner engagement, as assumed in the ICAP framework ([Chi & Wylie, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib9)). The results can provide additional perspectives on the beneficial use of this technology, particularly with regard to the achievement of specific learning objectives.

2. Theory
---------

In order to approach the topic of IVR-based learning from a theoretical perspective, it is important to first consider the basic paradigms of relevant theories. Learning is often characterized as a lasting change in behavior resulting from practice or experience, but it is important to note that learning does not always result in behavioral change ([Lachman, 1997](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib20)). In this regard, the most important goal of educational processes is the acquisition of declarative and procedural knowledge that individuals need to solve specific problems ([Anderson, 1976](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib1), [2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib2); [Renkl, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib38)). Both procedural and [declarative knowledge](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/declarative-knowledge "Learn more about declarative knowledge from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") play an important role in how we understand and interact with our environment. Although declarative and procedural knowledge are presented as two separate domains, they are interrelated ([Anderson & Krathwohl, 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib3)). In [cognitive psychology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/cognitive-psychology "Learn more about cognitive psychology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), declarative knowledge refers to knowledge of facts and events, knowledge of complex relationships, knowledge of rules and constraining conditions, and knowledge of [semantic relationships](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/semantic-relationship "Learn more about semantic relationships from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Renkl, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib38)). Procedural knowledge, on the other hand, focuses on application, such as calculating a [mathematical problem](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/mathematical-problem "Learn more about mathematical problem from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or writing a textual argument. Procedural knowledge does not need to be reconstructed explicitly and consciously ([Anderson & Krathwohl, 2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib3)). Thus, procedural knowledge represents the ability to perform certain actions purposefully and effectively or to reproduce sequences of actions in a verbalized form. Almost all [learning and memory](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/learning-and-memory "Learn more about learning and memory from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") phenomena are procedural. Purely declarative knowledge is the exception rather than the rule (ten Berge & van Hezewijk, 1999). It can be assumed that procedural knowledge builds on declarative knowledge (ibid.). Higher levels of knowledge are usually characterized by an increasing degree of proceduralization, so that in the course of this process knowledge turns into ability. Therefore, procedural knowledge can also be identified with skill ([Renkl, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib38)). Both types of knowledge can refer to technical, domain-specific knowledge and also have interdisciplinary components. In general, it should be noted that knowledge acquisition is not only about the quantitative acquisition of individual knowledge elements, but also about the quality of knowledge and the networking of knowledge structures (ibid.). Due to the possibility of computer-based simulation and interaction, [IVR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/immersive-virtual-reality "Learn more about IVR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is considered to promote specific knowledge and practical skills ([Radianti et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib37)).

According to the _Cognitive Affective Model of Immersive Learning_ (CAMIL), the user's sense of presence and agency are critical affordances for learning in [IVR](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/immersive-virtual-reality "Learn more about IVR from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), with immersion, control factors, and representational fidelity able to facilitate these affordances ([Makransky & Petersen, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib26)). In particular, the importance of _agency_ in the context of IVR-based learning cannot be overstated. It refers to the learner's sense of control, [autonomy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/autonomy "Learn more about autonomy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and ability to take meaningful action within the virtual environment. The perception of agency can increase engagement in the virtual environment, contributing to a heightened sense of presence and a [deeper understanding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/deep-understanding "Learn more about deeper understanding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the subject matter. In this context, a suitable system for categorising IVR-based learning in terms of different levels of engagement is [Chi and Wylie's (2014)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib9) ICAP framework, which describes four different modes of [learner engagement](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/learner-engagement "Learn more about learner engagement from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"): _Passive Receiving_, _Active Manipulating_, _Constructive Generating_ and _Interactive Dialoguing_. At the passive receiving level, learners are mere recipients of information, engaging in activities such as watching lectures or reading without active participation. Passive receiving refers to a low-level engagement where learners are passive recipients of information. In this mode, learners do not actively process or interact with the content but rather absorb it without significant cognitive involvement. Moving up the hierarchy, at the active manipulating level, learners process information and participate in basic interactions, fostering a more engaged [learning experience](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/learning-experience "Learn more about learning experience from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Active manipulating involves a higher level of cognitive engagement, where learners actively manipulate information, apply concepts, or perform hands-on activities. This level requires learners to go beyond passive reception and actively interact with the learning materials. Constructive generating emphasizes the active construction of knowledge, encouraging learners to integrate concepts and develop problem-solving skills. In this mode of engagement, learners actively create knowledge by integrating and synthesizing information to form a deeper understanding. It goes beyond basic interaction and requires learners to generate new insights or solutions. Group work, projects, and simulations are examples of constructive activities. Finally, the interactive dialoging level represents the pinnacle, where learners not only construct knowledge, but also share and discuss it with others through [group discussions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/group-discussion "Learn more about group discussions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), cooperative learning, or online interactions. This mode represents the highest level of engagement in the ICAP model. It involves not only constructing knowledge but also sharing, discussing, and refining it through interactions with peers. Interactive dialoging emphasizes [collaborative learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/collaborative-learning "Learn more about collaborative learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the social construction of knowledge.

The ICAP framework emphasizes the critical link between interactivity and constructiveness in learning activities and highlights their central role in enhancing [cognitive processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/cognitive-process "Learn more about cognitive processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and comprehension. It provides a solid foundation for designing learning environments that actively engage learners and enhance their cognitive skills. The corresponding hypothesis predicts that the more engaged learners are in the learning environment, the more they will learn ([Chi & Wylie, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib9)). Following Chi and Wylie's (ibid) category system, we have simplified the basic modes of IVR-based learning environments to distinguish passive forms of engagement from more active forms. The resulting dichotomous distinction is between _IVR environments with passive learner engagement_ (e.g. viewing a digital artefact) and _IVR environments with active learner engagement_ (e.g. bidirectional interaction with an IVR environment). Given this context, scenarios surpassing passive observation can be categorized as learning environments with active learner engagement. This is due to the necessity for proactive participation from the learner, thereby stimulating heightened cognitive processes. The resulting classification of IVR-based learning environments can be used to [design learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/learning-design "Learn more about design learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") scenarios that meet specific learning objectives. In the present study, we will use these dichotomous categories to compare basic types of learning environments according to the corresponding learner engagement ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#fig1)).

### 2.1. Reviews on teaching and learning with IVR

To obtain a general overview of previous [systematic literature reviews](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic literature reviews from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") on the topic of “IVR in education”, a systematic search for systematic literature reviews on the learning-related effectiveness of [IVR technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/virtual-reality-technology "Learn more about IVR technologies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was conducted using the educational and psychological databases _APA PsycArticles_, _APA PsycInfo_, _Education Resources_ _[Information Center](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/computer-science/information-centre "Learn more about Information Center from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ (ERIC), and _Education Resources Information Center_. To ensure sufficient comparability of study results, only reviews from 2010 onwards that were embedded in formal educational contexts and based on the use of _head-mounted displays_ (HMDs) were included, as this technology is the most common form of IVR use. Based on this specification, five relevant reviews were identified ([Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#tbl1)).

Table 1. Selected reviews on learning with IVR technologies as of 2010.

| Authors | Number of studies included | Study focus |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Billingsley et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib4) | 7 | Teacher training: Effects of IVR on understanding, knowledge, skills and attitudes. |
| [Concannon et al. (2019)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib12) | 119 | Areas of application, learning outcomes, and conceptual and theoretical rationale for the use of IVR in post-secondary education. |
| [Hamilton et al. (2021)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib15) | 29 | Comparison of learning outcomes between IVR users and control groups (including desktop VR, PowerPoint, video). |
| [Jensen and Konradsen (2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib18) | 21 | Acquisition of affective, cognitive and psychomotor skills in the field of education. |
| [Radianti et al. (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib37) | 38 | Learning content and application areas of IVR in higher education. |

The identified reviews focus on different aspects of learning and different sectors of secondary and tertiary education (e.g., high school, university, vocational training) ([Concannon et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib12); [Hamilton et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib15); [Radianti et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib37)). Although the varying focus of the studies does not allow for a definitive conclusion on the effects of learning with IVR, a number of findings can be derived regarding the effectiveness of learning in terms of achieving relevant learning goals.

Overall, the use of IVR technologies tends to be superior to non-immersive technologies in terms of recall of abstract and conceptual information and acquisition of procedural knowledge ([Hamilton et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib15)). However, the effects of these studies have reported mixed results ([Concannon et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib12); [Hamilton et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib15); [Jensen & Konradsen, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib18)). [Jensen and Konradsen (2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949678024000035#bib18) note inconsistent findings regarding the development of cognitive skills when using IVR for education and training. Some of the studies reviewed here find an increase in cognitive skills, while other studies find no positive effects of IVR-based learning, especially when compared to desktop-based training and other learning methods or media. However, the use of IVR is beneficial when the focus is on visual and spatial perception, such as in safety or medical

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Virtual reality (VR) enhances learning by immersing students in interactive environments, resulting in improved engagement and retention, as demonstrated by studies showing positive learning outcomes.

Brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) allow direct communication between the brain and computers, facilitating personalized learning experiences and enabling real-time feedback to enhance cognitive engagement.

Adaptive learning platforms tailor educational content to individual student needs, optimizing learning paths and improving outcomes by responding dynamically to a learner’s progress and understanding.
Integrating technology in education significantly boosts student engagement by providing immersive and interactive experiences, making learning more appealing and effective.
Technology enables personalized learning experiences, adapting to diverse learning styles and paces, ensuring that each student receives the support they need to succeed.
